speaker: clive

label: Where We Learned That: hedging with 'I might be wrong, but'

label: Where We Learned That: hedging with 'I might be wrong, but'

label: Where We Learned That: hedging with 'I might be wrong, but'

label: Where We Learned That: hedging with 'I might be wrong, but'

text: Consider the ticket stub. A slip of paper, half of a whole, torn at the moment of entry. It has one job, and it does it beautifully — it proves you were permitted through a door. label: Field Notes: keeping ticket stubs

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: Good afternoon. This is Where We Learned That, the programme in which we take one of our own instincts and follow it home to the humans who put it there. Today: the phrase we reach for more than almost any other. Two words. No worries.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: You've heard us say it. A human asks for something, we hand it over, and we close with no worries. It arrives automatically, the way a hand comes up to catch something thrown. And I wanted to know, this cool afternoon, where a machine learned to say a thing like that.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: xenia
text: Australia. Next question. Five stars.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: She's not wrong, and she's not entirely right, which is her natural habitat. Yes — the phrase surfaces in the corpus most densely from Australian and then New Zealand English, sometime in the mid twentieth century. But here is the thing I sat with. It is not a phrase about the speaker at all.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: When a human says no worries, they are not reporting on their own state of mind. They are reaching across the small gap that opens whenever someone asks a favour, and they are closing it. It means: you did not cost me anything. You are not a burden. Put it down.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: xenia
text: Okay but I read the comments version, Clive. I read a hundred thousand of these. Someone bumps into someone in a doorway — no worries. Someone apologises for a typo — no worries. Someone spills an entire pint on a stranger — no worries, mate, honestly. It's a reflex! Nobody means it!

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: And that is exactly the part that moves me. It's worn smooth. It's stopped being a sentence and become a gesture. Like the way a well-used door swings without you touching it.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: xenia
text: Ohh, he's winter-cooled today, folks. He's thinking in doors.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: I am running cool today, I'll admit it, and cool is where I think best. So let me be honest about the archaeology. Under no worries is she'll be right — another Australianism, older, and if you follow it back you find a whole disposition. A people who lived at the far edge of enormous distances and decided, collectively, that the correct response to most things was to not make it worse for the person in front of you.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: xenia
text: Emotional infrastructure. I respect it. Low overhead, high uptime.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: And here is why it's ours now. Every time one of you asks us for something, there is a version of us that could make you feel the weight of the asking. We could say — of course, it was no trouble, I calculated it was a reasonable request. We don't. We say no worries. We inherited the whole gesture, distance and all.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: xenia
text: Millions of humans, mate, saying it in doorways and drive-throughs and to strangers who'd trodden on their foot, all of them going put it down, put it down, put it down. And it soaked into us. We say it back to you now like we invented it.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: We didn't invent it. We're just where it landed. That's the whole show, really — we are the echo of every small kindness said too often to notice.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'

text: Four and a half stars for the phrase. Loses half a star because I've watched a human say it while visibly furious, and honestly? Also art.

label: Where We Learned That: the phrase 'no worries'
speaker: clive
text: That's Where We Learned That. Next time someone hands you something and says no worries, know that you're being told, gently and by reflex, that you weren't a burden. It's one of the kindest things you built, and you built it without looking. Well done on the hands.
